rationale: This structure uses a hybrid classification model, organizing subspecialties by material class (e.g., Metals & Alloys), physical scale (e.g., 2D Materials), function (e.g., Energy Materials), and methodology (e.g., Computational Materials). This reflects how interdisciplinary teams and researchers self-identify in modern materials science. Broad functional categories like 'Energy Materials' and 'Electronic & Photonic Materials' were created to capture the largest, most active areas of research, preventing them from being scattered across multiple material classes. To manage overlap, a soft hierarchy is implied: a paper on a material for a specific application (e.g., a polymer for a battery) belongs in the application category ('Energy Materials') over the material class category ('Polymers & Soft Matter').

subspecialties.name: Energy Materials
subspecialties.tagline: Materials for energy generation, conversion, and storage.
covers: Research on materials central to energy technologies.
Batteries, photovoltaics, fuel cells, thermoelectrics, supercapacitors, materials for catalysis.
explicitly_excludes: Fundamental electronic properties not tied to a device → Electronic & Photonic Materials

subspecialties.name: Electronic & Photonic Materials
subspecialties.tagline: Materials governing the flow of electrons and light.
covers: Fundamental properties and processing of semiconductors, dielectrics, and optical materials.
Semiconductors, transistors, LEDs, lasers, optical fibers, plasmonics, ferroelectrics.
explicitly_excludes: Materials primarily for energy conversion/storage → Energy Materials
Purely magnetic phenomena → Magnetic Materials & Spintronics

subspecialties.name: Nanomaterials
subspecialties.tagline: Synthesis and properties of materials at the nanoscale.
covers: Materials where nanoscale effects dominate properties.
Nanoparticles, quantum dots, nanotubes, nanowires, nanocomposites.
explicitly_excludes: Graphene and other single-layer materials → 2D Materials & Heterostructures

subspecialties.name: Biomaterials
subspecialties.tagline: Materials designed to interact with biological systems.
covers: Development and application of materials for medical and biological purposes.
Tissue engineering, drug delivery, medical implants, biosensors, biocompatible coatings.
explicitly_excludes: Fundamental polymer science without a biological interface → Polymers & Soft Matter

subspecialties.name: Polymers & Soft Matter
subspecialties.tagline: The science of large molecules and easily deformable matter.
covers: Synthesis, physics, and characterization of polymers and complex fluids.
Polymer chemistry, polymer physics, colloids, gels, liquid crystals, self-assembly.
explicitly_excludes: Polymers for specific applications → relevant functional category (e.g., Energy Materials, Biomaterials)

subspecialties.name: Metals & Alloys
covers: Physical and mechanical metallurgy, from fundamentals to alloy design.
Steels, superalloys, high-entropy alloys, intermetallics, phase transformations, corrosion.
explicitly_excludes: Thin film deposition for electronics → Electronic & Photonic Materials

subspecialties.name: 2D Materials & Heterostructures
subspecialties.tagline: Exploring the physics and chemistry of atomically thin materials.
covers: Synthesis, characterization, and theory of 2D materials and their stacked assemblies.
Graphene, transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs), MXenes, van der Waals heterostructures.

subspecialties.name: Structural Materials & Composites
subspecialties.tagline: Materials engineered for mechanical performance and load-bearing applications.
covers: Design and mechanics of materials where strength, toughness, and durability are primary.
Fiber-reinforced composites, lightweight alloys, advanced ceramics, mechanical testing.

subspecialties.name: Ceramics & Glasses
subspecialties.tagline: Synthesis, structure, and properties of inorganic, non-metallic solids.
covers: Processing and characterization of ceramic and amorphous materials.
Oxides, nitrides, carbides, glass science, cements, electroceramics.
explicitly_excludes: Ceramics for specific applications → relevant functional category (e.g., Energy Materials, Biomaterials)

subspecialties.name: Computational Materials Science
subspecialties.tagline: Using simulation and data to discover and design materials.
covers: Development and application of computational methods for materials.
Density functional theory (DFT), molecular dynamics (MD), phase-field modeling, materials informatics.
explicitly_excludes: Papers that primarily report experimental results with minor computational support.

subspecialties.name: Magnetic Materials & Spintronics
subspecialties.tagline: Controlling magnetism and electron spin for future technologies.
covers: Fundamental and applied studies of magnetic phenomena in materials.
Permanent magnets, magnetic memory, spintronics, multiferroics, magnetic cooling.
explicitly_excludes: Non-magnetic electronic properties → Electronic & Photonic Materials

subspecialties.name: Surfaces, Interfaces & Coatings
subspecialties.tagline: The science and engineering of material boundaries.
covers: Phenomena occurring at surfaces and interfaces, and the design of functional coatings.
Thin film growth, surface science, tribology, corrosion resistance, wetting.

uncovered_concerns: A separate category for 'Materials Characterization' was considered but excluded because characterization techniques (e.g., microscopy, spectroscopy) are tools used across all subspecialties, rather than a distinct field of study for most papers.

A category for 'Quantum Materials' was considered but its topics are well-distributed across 'Electronic & Photonic Materials', 'Magnetic Materials & Spintronics', and '2D Materials', so a separate category felt redundant at this level.
